title: B-tree indexes don't help with LIKE '%prefix' queries
GIN indexes are slow to update but fast to query
body:
GIN (Generalized Inverted Index) indexes are optimized for containment queries but have significantly slower write performance than B-tree.

```sql
-- GIN is great for:
CREATE INDEX idx_tags ON articles USING gin (tags);  -- array containment
CREATE INDEX idx_doc ON documents USING gin (to_tsvector('english', body));  -- full-text
CREATE INDEX idx_data ON events USING gin (metadata);  -- JSONB containment

-- But writes are ~3-5x slower than B-tree
-- Mitigation: use fastupdate (default ON)
CREATE INDEX idx_tags ON articles USING gin (tags) WITH (fastupdate = on);

-- fastupdate batches index updates into a pending list
-- Trades read performance for write speed
-- Pending list is flushed during VACUUM or when it gets large

-- Check pending pages
SELECT * FROM pg_stat_user_indexes WHERE indexrelname = 'idx_tags';

-- For high-write tables, consider:
-- 1. Partial GIN index on frequently queried subset
CREATE INDEX idx_active_tags ON articles USING gin (tags)
    WHERE status = 'published';
-- 2. Defer index creation to off-peak hours
-- 3. Use CONCURRENTLY for zero-downtime index builds
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_tags ON articles USING gin (tags);
```

title: BRIN indexes only work well on physically ordered data
body:
BRIN (Block Range Index) indexes store min/max values per block range. They're tiny but only effective when data is physically correlated with the indexed column.

```sql
-- GOOD: timestamp on append-only table (naturally ordered)
CREATE INDEX idx_events_ts ON events USING brin (created_at);
-- Index size: ~0.01% of table size vs ~15% for B-tree

-- BAD: random UUIDs (no physical correlation)
CREATE INDEX idx_events_id ON events USING brin (id);  -- useless!

-- Check correlation (1.0 = perfectly correlated)
SELECT correlation
FROM pg_stats
WHERE tablename = 'events' AND attname = 'created_at';
-- correlation > 0.9 = BRIN works great
-- correlation < 0.5 = BRIN is nearly useless

-- BRIN with custom pages_per_range
CREATE INDEX idx_events_ts ON events USING brin (created_at)
    WITH (pages_per_range = 32);  -- default is 128
-- Smaller range = more precise but larger index

-- BRIN is ideal for:
-- 1. Time-series data (logs, events, metrics)
-- 2. Auto-incrementing IDs
-- 3. Partitioned tables where each partition is ordered
```

BRIN indexes are 100-1000x smaller than B-tree indexes. Use them for large, append-mostly tables with naturally ordered data.

title: JSONB @> operator uses GIN index but -> does not
JSONB null is different from SQL NULL
body:
JSONB has its own `null` value that is distinct from SQL `NULL`. This causes confusing query results.

```sql
-- These are different!
SELECT '{"key": null}'::jsonb->>'key';     -- returns NULL (SQL)
SELECT '{"key": null}'::jsonb->'key';      -- returns 'null' (JSONB)
SELECT '{}'::jsonb->>'key';                 -- returns NULL (key missing)

-- Comparing JSONB null
SELECT '{"key": null}'::jsonb->'key' = 'null'::jsonb;  -- true
SELECT '{"key": null}'::jsonb->>'key' IS NULL;         -- true!

-- Checking if key exists vs is null
SELECT '{"key": null}'::jsonb ? 'key';     -- true (key exists)
SELECT '{}'::jsonb ? 'key';                -- false (key missing)

-- To distinguish "key is null" from "key is missing":
SELECT
    data ? 'email' AS key_exists,
    data->>'email' IS NULL AS value_is_null,
    CASE
        WHEN NOT (data ? 'email') THEN 'missing'
        WHEN data->'email' = 'null'::jsonb THEN 'json_null'
        ELSE data->>'email'
    END AS status
FROM users;
```

This distinction is critical when migrating between relational columns and JSONB. A missing key and a JSON null key look the same with `->>` but mean different things.

title: Full-text search requires explicit tsvector column for performance
pgbouncer transaction mode breaks session-level features
body:
PgBouncer in transaction pooling mode (the most common) breaks any feature that depends on session state: prepared statements, temp tables, advisory locks, and SET commands.

```sql
-- These break in transaction pooling mode:
PREPARE my_query AS SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1;
CREATE TEMP TABLE tmp_results (id int);
SELECT pg_advisory_lock(123);
SET search_path TO myschema;
LISTEN channel_name;
DECLARE cursor_name CURSOR FOR SELECT ...;

-- Fix 1: Use session pooling for features that need session state
-- pgbouncer.ini:
-- [databases]
-- mydb = host=localhost pool_mode=session  # per-database override

-- Fix 2: Use DISCARD ALL at transaction boundaries
-- application.conf:
-- connectionInitSql = "DISCARD ALL"

-- Fix 3: Use protocol-level prepared statements instead
-- In application code (e.g., asyncpg):
-- conn.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1", user_id)
-- asyncpg handles prepared statements transparently
```

```ini
# pgbouncer.ini recommended settings
[pgbouncer]
pool_mode = transaction
max_client_conn = 1000
default_pool_size = 25
reserve_pool_size = 5
server_reset_query = DISCARD ALL
```

Transaction pooling gives the best connection reuse but requires awareness of session state limitations.

title: READ COMMITTED isolation level allows non-repeatable reads
Deadlocks from inconsistent lock ordering in concurrent updates
VACUUM does not reclaim disk space without VACUUM FULL
body:
Regular `VACUUM` marks dead tuples as reusable but does not shrink the table file on disk. Only `VACUUM FULL` reclaims space, but it locks the table.

```sql
-- Regular VACUUM — marks space reusable, no lock
VACUUM users;  -- dead tuples → reusable space (no disk shrink)

-- VACUUM FULL — rewrites entire table, reclaims disk
VACUUM FULL users;  -- ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock! Table offline!

-- Check bloat
SELECT
    schemaname, tablename,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(schemaname || '.' || tablename)) AS total_size,
    n_dead_tup,
    n_live_tup,
    ROUND(n_dead_tup::numeric / NULLIF(n_live_tup, 0) * 100, 2) AS dead_pct
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
ORDER BY n_dead_tup DESC;

-- Better alternative: pg_repack (no lock)
-- pg_repack --table=users --no-superuser-check mydb

-- Prevent bloat with aggressive autovacuum
ALTER TABLE high_churn_table SET (
    autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor = 0.01,  -- default 0.2
    autovacuum_vacuum_threshold = 50,       -- default 50
    autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor = 0.01
);
```

Never run `VACUUM FULL` on production during business hours. Use `pg_repack` for online table repacking without ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock.

title: Table partitioning does not automatically use partition pruning
body:
PostgreSQL only prunes partitions when the query directly filters on the partition key with a constant value.

```sql
-- Create partitioned table
CREATE TABLE events (
    id bigint GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY,
    created_at timestamptz NOT NULL,
    data jsonb
) PARTITION BY RANGE (created_at);

CREATE TABLE events_2024_01 PARTITION OF events
    FOR VALUES FROM ('2024-01-01') TO ('2024-02-01');
CREATE TABLE events_2024_02 PARTITION OF events
    FOR VALUES FROM ('2024-02-01') TO ('2024-03-01');

-- GOOD: partition pruning works (constant filter)
SELECT * FROM events WHERE created_at >= '2024-01-15' AND created_at < '2024-02-01';
-- Only scans events_2024_01

-- BAD: partition pruning fails (function call)
SELECT * FROM events WHERE created_at >= NOW() - INTERVAL '7 days';
-- Scans ALL partitions! (NOW() is not a constant at plan time)

-- Fix: use IMMUTABLE wrapper or explicit date
SELECT * FROM events
WHERE created_at >= CURRENT_DATE - 7
  AND created_at < CURRENT_DATE + 1;

-- Verify pruning with EXPLAIN
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM events WHERE created_at >= '2024-01-15';
-- Look for: "Partitions removed: N" or check which partitions are scanned
```

Enable `enable_partition_pruning = on` (default) and check `EXPLAIN` output to verify pruning is working.

title: CTEs are optimization fences before PostgreSQL 12
body:
Before PostgreSQL 12, CTEs (WITH queries) were always materialized, preventing the planner from pushing predicates into them. In 12+, CTEs can be inlined.

```sql
-- In PostgreSQL 11: CTE materializes ALL users, then filters
WITH active_users AS (
    SELECT * FROM users WHERE status = 'active'
)
SELECT * FROM active_users WHERE age > 30;
-- Reads ALL active users into memory, then filters age

-- In PostgreSQL 12+: CTE is inlined (equivalent to subquery)
-- Same query is optimized to: SELECT * FROM users WHERE status = 'active' AND age > 30

-- Force materialization when needed (12+)
WITH active_users AS MATERIALIZED (
    SELECT * FROM users WHERE status = 'active'
)
SELECT * FROM active_users a1
JOIN active_users a2 ON a1.id = a2.manager_id;
-- MATERIALIZED prevents computing the CTE twice

-- Force inlining (12+)
WITH active_users AS NOT MATERIALIZED (
    SELECT * FROM users WHERE status = 'active'
)
SELECT * FROM active_users WHERE age > 30;
```

After upgrading to 12+, CTEs with side effects (INSERT/UPDATE) are still materialized. Use `MATERIALIZED` when the CTE is referenced multiple times to avoid recomputation.

title: EXPLAIN ANALYZE actually executes the query (including writes)
body:
`EXPLAIN ANALYZE` runs the full query to get actual timings. If it's a DELETE or UPDATE, the changes happen for real.

```sql
-- DANGER: this actually deletes rows!
EXPLAIN ANALYZE DELETE FROM logs WHERE created_at < '2020-01-01';

-- SAFE: wrap in a transaction and rollback
BEGIN;
EXPLAIN ANALYZE DELETE FROM logs WHERE created_at < '2020-01-01';
ROLLBACK;  -- undo the delete

-- SAFE: use EXPLAIN without ANALYZE for write queries
EXPLAIN DELETE FROM logs WHERE created_at < '2020-01-01';
-- Shows plan estimates without executing

-- Useful EXPLAIN options
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT)
    SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = 'test@example.com';

-- Key things to look for:
-- Seq Scan vs Index Scan (is index being used?)
-- actual time vs estimated rows (bad estimates?)
-- Buffers: shared hit vs read (cache hit ratio)
-- Sort Method: quicksort vs external merge (memory pressure)

-- JSON format for programmatic analysis
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT JSON)
    SELECT * FROM large_table WHERE id = 1;
```

Always wrap `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` for DML statements in a transaction with `ROLLBACK` to prevent unintended data changes.

title: Row-level security policies are bypassed by table owners
Trigger fires once per statement by default, not per row
Adding a column with a default value locks the table in old PostgreSQL
NOT IN with NULL values returns no rows
body:
If the subquery in `NOT IN` returns any NULL value, the entire condition evaluates to NULL (not TRUE), so no rows match.

```sql
-- Setup
CREATE TABLE orders (id int, user_id int);
CREATE TABLE blacklist (user_id int);  -- contains: 1, 2, NULL

-- BROKEN: returns 0 rows because blacklist contains NULL!
SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id NOT IN (SELECT user_id FROM blacklist);

-- Why: NOT IN (1, 2, NULL) → NOT (user_id=1 OR user_id=2 OR user_id=NULL)
-- user_id=NULL is UNKNOWN, so the OR is UNKNOWN, NOT UNKNOWN = UNKNOWN
-- No rows satisfy UNKNOWN condition

-- Fix 1: Filter NULLs
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE user_id NOT IN (SELECT user_id FROM blacklist WHERE user_id IS NOT NULL);

-- Fix 2: Use NOT EXISTS (preferred)
SELECT * FROM orders o
WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM blacklist b WHERE b.user_id = o.user_id);

-- Fix 3: Use LEFT JOIN / anti-join
SELECT o.* FROM orders o
LEFT JOIN blacklist b ON o.user_id = b.user_id
WHERE b.user_id IS NULL;
```

`NOT EXISTS` is almost always better than `NOT IN` — it handles NULLs correctly and often performs better with proper indexes.

title: Array operations with NULL elements behave unexpectedly
body:
PostgreSQL arrays can contain NULL elements, which causes surprising behavior with operators like `@>`, `ANY`, and array functions.

```sql
-- NULL in array containment
SELECT ARRAY[1, NULL, 3] @> ARRAY[NULL];  -- NULL (not true!)
SELECT NULL = ANY(ARRAY[1, NULL, 3]);      -- NULL (not true!)

-- Check if value is in array
SELECT 1 = ANY(ARRAY[1, 2, 3]);           -- true
SELECT 4 = ANY(ARRAY[1, 2, 3]);           -- false
SELECT NULL = ANY(ARRAY[1, 2, 3]);        -- NULL

-- Safely check array membership
SELECT COALESCE(1 = ANY(ARRAY[1, NULL, 3]), false);  -- true
SELECT COALESCE(4 = ANY(ARRAY[1, NULL, 3]), false);  -- false

-- Array functions and NULLs
SELECT array_length(ARRAY[1, NULL, 3], 1);  -- 3 (NULLs are counted)
SELECT array_remove(ARRAY[1, NULL, 3], NULL);  -- {1,3}
SELECT array_position(ARRAY[1, NULL, 3], NULL);  -- NULL (can't find NULL)

-- Unnest preserves NULLs
SELECT unnest(ARRAY[1, NULL, 3]);  -- 1, NULL, 3

-- Safe array aggregation
SELECT array_agg(x) FROM unnest(ARRAY[1, NULL, 3]) x WHERE x IS NOT NULL;
-- {1, 3}
```

title: Window functions execute after WHERE but before ORDER BY/LIMIT
body:
Window functions are computed after `WHERE`, `GROUP BY`, and `HAVING` are applied, but before the final `ORDER BY` and `LIMIT`. This means you can't filter by window function results directly.

```sql
-- WRONG: can't use window function in WHERE
SELECT *, row_number() OVER (ORDER BY created_at) AS rn
FROM orders
WHERE rn <= 10;  -- ERROR: column "rn" does not exist

-- Fix: use subquery or CTE
SELECT * FROM (
    SELECT *, row_number() OVER (ORDER BY created_at) AS rn
    FROM orders
) sub
WHERE rn <= 10;

-- Common patterns:
-- Top N per group
SELECT * FROM (
    SELECT *,
        row_number() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY created_at DESC) AS rn
    FROM orders
) sub
WHERE rn <= 3;  -- last 3 orders per user

-- Running total
SELECT
    id,
    amount,
    SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY created_at) AS running_total,
    AVG(amount) OVER (ORDER BY created_at ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS moving_avg
FROM transactions;
```

Window function execution order: FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → window functions → ORDER BY → LIMIT.

title: CREATE INDEX blocks writes unless you use CONCURRENTLY
UPDATE with JOIN syntax differs from standard SQL in PostgreSQL
body:
PostgreSQL uses `FROM` clause for UPDATE joins instead of the `UPDATE ... JOIN` syntax used by MySQL/SQL Server.

```sql
-- MySQL/SQL Server syntax (does NOT work in PostgreSQL)
UPDATE orders o
JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.id
SET o.email = u.email;

-- PostgreSQL syntax
UPDATE orders o
SET email = u.email
FROM users u
WHERE o.user_id = u.id;

-- Multiple table join
UPDATE orders o
SET
    email = u.email,
    discount = p.discount_rate
FROM users u
JOIN promotions p ON u.tier = p.tier
WHERE o.user_id = u.id
  AND o.status = 'pending';

-- DELETE with join also uses USING
DELETE FROM orders o
USING users u
WHERE o.user_id = u.id
  AND u.status = 'deleted';

-- Subquery alternative (works everywhere)
UPDATE orders
SET email = (SELECT email FROM users WHERE users.id = orders.user_id)
WHERE user_id IN (SELECT id FROM users WHERE status = 'active');
```

The `FROM` clause syntax is PostgreSQL-specific but more readable for complex joins. The subquery approach is more portable across databases.

title: SERIAL is not recommended anymore — use GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY
Trigger on partitioned table requires separate triggers per partition before PG 13
body:
Before PostgreSQL 13, triggers defined on a partitioned table were NOT automatically created on partitions. You had to create them on each partition individually.

```sql
-- PostgreSQL 13+: trigger on parent propagates to all partitions
CREATE TRIGGER audit_events
    AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE OR DELETE ON events
    FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION audit_trigger();
-- Automatically applies to events_2024_01, events_2024_02, etc.

-- PostgreSQL 11-12: must create trigger on EACH partition
CREATE TRIGGER audit_events_2024_01
    AFTER INSERT ON events_2024_01
    FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION audit_trigger();
CREATE TRIGGER audit_events_2024_02
    AFTER INSERT ON events_2024_02
    FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION audit_trigger();
-- Forget one partition and you miss audit data!

-- Automate for pre-13 with event trigger
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION auto_add_triggers()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
DECLARE obj record;
BEGIN
    FOR obj IN SELECT * FROM pg_event_trigger_ddl_commands()
        WHERE command_tag = 'CREATE TABLE'
    LOOP
        -- Add triggers to new partitions automatically
        EXECUTE format('CREATE TRIGGER ... ON %s ...', obj.object_identity);
    END LOOP;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;
```

title: GiST index for range types and spatial queries
body:
GiST (Generalized Search Tree) indexes support range types, geometric data, and exclusion constraints — things B-tree cannot handle.

```sql
-- Range queries with GiST
CREATE TABLE reservations (
    id int PRIMARY KEY,
    room_id int,
    during tstzrange NOT NULL
);

CREATE INDEX idx_reservations_during ON reservations USING gist (during);

-- Find overlapping reservations
SELECT * FROM reservations
WHERE during && tstzrange('2024-01-15', '2024-01-20');

-- Exclusion constraint: prevent double-booking
ALTER TABLE reservations ADD CONSTRAINT no_double_booking
    EXCLUDE USING gist (room_id WITH =, during WITH &&);

-- Now this fails:
INSERT INTO reservations (room_id, during)
VALUES (1, '[2024-01-15, 2024-01-20)');
INSERT INTO reservations (room_id, during)
VALUES (1, '[2024-01-18, 2024-01-22)');  -- ERROR: conflicting row

-- GiST for spatial (PostGIS)
CREATE INDEX idx_location ON stores USING gist (location);
SELECT * FROM stores
WHERE ST_DWithin(location, ST_MakePoint(-73.97, 40.77)::geography, 1000);
-- Finds stores within 1km
```

GiST indexes are essential for range types (`int4range`, `tstzrange`, etc.), PostGIS geometry, and exclusion constraints.

title: COUNT(*) is slow on large tables in PostgreSQL
NOTIFY/LISTEN messages are lost if no one is listening
body:
PostgreSQL's LISTEN/NOTIFY is fire-and-forget. If no client is listening when NOTIFY fires, the message is permanently lost.

```sql
-- Publisher
NOTIFY new_order, '{"order_id": 123}';
-- or
SELECT pg_notify('new_order', '{"order_id": 123}');

-- Subscriber (must be listening BEFORE the notify)
LISTEN new_order;
-- Then poll with: SELECT * FROM pg_notification_queue;

-- Messages sent before LISTEN are lost!
-- Messages sent during subscriber disconnect are lost!
```

```python
# Python listener with asyncpg
import asyncpg
import asyncio

async def listen_for_events():
    conn = await asyncpg.connect('postgresql://...')
    await conn.add_listener('new_order', lambda conn, pid, channel, payload:
        print(f"Got: {payload}")
    )
    # Keep connection alive
    while True:
        await asyncio.sleep(1)
```

For reliable messaging, use LISTEN/NOTIFY as a trigger mechanism but store events in a table (outbox pattern). The listener reads from the table on startup to catch missed events.

title: Foreign key constraints prevent concurrent partition management
JSONB arrays are not PostgreSQL arrays and use different operators
body:
JSONB array operations use different syntax and functions than native PostgreSQL arrays. Mixing them up causes errors.

```sql
-- JSONB array
SELECT '{"tags": ["python", "sql"]}'::jsonb -> 'tags';  -- ["python", "sql"] (jsonb)

-- Check if JSONB array contains a value
SELECT '{"tags": ["python", "sql"]}'::jsonb -> 'tags' ? 'python';  -- true
SELECT '{"tags": ["python", "sql"]}'::jsonb @> '{"tags": ["python"]}';  -- true

-- PostgreSQL native array
SELECT ARRAY['python', 'sql'];  -- {python,sql} (text[])
SELECT 'python' = ANY(ARRAY['python', 'sql']);  -- true
SELECT ARRAY['python'] <@ ARRAY['python', 'sql'];  -- true

-- Convert JSONB array to PostgreSQL array
SELECT ARRAY(
    SELECT jsonb_array_elements_text('{"tags": ["python", "sql"]}'::jsonb -> 'tags')
);  -- {python,sql}

-- Unnest JSONB array
SELECT jsonb_array_elements_text('["a", "b", "c"]'::jsonb);
-- vs native array
SELECT unnest(ARRAY['a', 'b', 'c']);

-- Aggregate back to JSONB array
SELECT jsonb_agg(tag) FROM unnest(ARRAY['python', 'sql']) tag;
-- ["python", "sql"]
```

When modeling arrays, prefer native PostgreSQL arrays if values are simple and homogeneous. Use JSONB arrays for heterogeneous or nested data.

title: Long-running transactions prevent VACUUM from cleaning dead tuples
body:
Any open transaction prevents VACUUM from removing dead tuples that are newer than the transaction's snapshot, causing table bloat.

```sql
-- Find long-running transactions
SELECT pid, now() - xact_start AS duration, state, query
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE xact_start IS NOT NULL
  AND state != 'idle'
ORDER BY xact_start;

-- Find transactions holding back VACUUM
SELECT
    pid,
    backend_xmin,
    now() - xact_start AS age,
    query
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE backend_xmin IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY age(backend_xmin) DESC
LIMIT 5;

-- Kill problematic transactions
SELECT pg_terminate_backend(pid);

-- Set statement timeout to prevent runaway queries
ALTER DATABASE mydb SET statement_timeout = '60s';

-- Set idle-in-transaction timeout
ALTER DATABASE mydb SET idle_in_transaction_session_timeout = '300s';

-- Check bloat after fixing
SELECT schemaname, tablename, n_dead_tup,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_table_size(schemaname || '.' || tablename)) AS size
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
WHERE n_dead_tup > 10000
ORDER BY n_dead_tup DESC;
```

Set `idle_in_transaction_session_timeout` in production to automatically kill idle transactions. A forgotten `BEGIN` without `COMMIT` is a common source of bloat.

title: Partial indexes dramatically reduce index size and improve performance
body:
A partial index only indexes rows that match a WHERE condition. For tables where queries typically filter on a subset, this is much more efficient.

```sql
-- Full index: indexes ALL rows
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status ON orders (status);
-- If 95% of orders are 'completed', this index is mostly wasted space

-- Partial index: only indexes active orders
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_active ON orders (created_at)
    WHERE status IN ('pending', 'processing');
-- 20x smaller than full index, much faster to maintain

-- Query MUST match the partial index condition
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE status = 'pending' AND created_at > '2024-01-01';
-- Uses idx_orders_active

SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE status = 'completed' AND created_at > '2024-01-01';
-- Does NOT use idx_orders_active

-- Unique partial index for soft deletes
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_users_email_active ON users (email)
    WHERE deleted_at IS NULL;
-- Allows duplicate emails for deleted users, unique for active

-- Partial index for NULL filtering
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_unprocessed ON orders (id)
    WHERE processed_at IS NULL;
```

Partial indexes are especially powerful for queues, soft-delete tables, and any table where most queries target a small subset of rows.

title: pg_stat_statements reset loses all query performance history
body:
`pg_stat_statements_reset()` clears all accumulated statistics. Always snapshot before resetting.

```sql
-- Enable pg_stat_statements (requires restart)
-- postgresql.conf:
-- shared_preload_libraries = 'pg_stat_statements'
-- pg_stat_statements.track = all

-- Top queries by total time
SELECT
    query,
    calls,
    round(total_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS total_ms,
    round(mean_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS avg_ms,
    round((100 * total_exec_time / sum(total_exec_time) OVER ())::numeric, 2) AS pct
FROM pg_stat_statements
ORDER BY total_exec_time DESC
LIMIT 20;

-- Save snapshot before reset
CREATE TABLE pg_stat_snapshots AS
SELECT now() AS snapshot_time, *
FROM pg_stat_statements;

-- Now safe to reset
SELECT pg_stat_statements_reset();

-- Automate snapshots with pg_cron
SELECT cron.schedule('stat-snapshot', '0 * * * *',
    $$INSERT INTO pg_stat_snapshots SELECT now(), * FROM pg_stat_statements$$
);
```

pg_stat_statements is the single most important extension for PostgreSQL performance tuning. Install it on every production database.

title: tsquery operators are different from LIKE patterns
body:
Full-text search uses its own query language with `&` (AND), `|` (OR), `!` (NOT), and `<->` (followed by). Common mistakes come from mixing LIKE patterns with tsquery syntax.

```sql
-- WRONG: using LIKE syntax in tsquery
SELECT * FROM articles
WHERE search_vector @@ to_tsquery('database%');  -- ERROR!

-- CORRECT: use :* for prefix matching
SELECT * FROM articles
WHERE search_vector @@ to_tsquery('english', 'datab:*');

-- tsquery operators
-- AND: &
SELECT to_tsquery('english', 'python & database');  -- both words
-- OR: |
SELECT to_tsquery('english', 'python | ruby');  -- either word
-- NOT: !
SELECT to_tsquery('english', 'python & !ruby');  -- python but not ruby
-- Phrase (adjacent words): <->
SELECT to_tsquery('english', 'machine <-> learning');  -- exact phrase
-- Distance: <N>
SELECT to_tsquery('english', 'machine <2> learning');  -- within 2 words

-- Safe user input with websearch_to_tsquery (11+)
SELECT websearch_to_tsquery('english', 'python -ruby "machine learning"');
-- Converts Google-like syntax to tsquery
-- Result: 'python' & !'ruby' & 'machin' <-> 'learn'

-- Use plainto_tsquery for simple word matching (no operators)
SELECT plainto_tsquery('english', 'machine learning database');
-- Result: 'machin' & 'learn' & 'databas'
```

title: RETURNING clause works with INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE
body:
PostgreSQL's `RETURNING` clause lets you get the affected rows back without a separate SELECT. It's essential for efficient upsert patterns.

```sql
-- Insert and get generated values
INSERT INTO users (name, email)
VALUES ('Alice', 'alice@example.com')
RETURNING id, created_at;
-- Returns: id=1, created_at=2024-01-15 10:30:00

-- Update and get old + new values
UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100
WHERE id = 1 AND balance >= 100
RETURNING id, balance AS new_balance;
-- Returns the row only if update succeeded (balance was sufficient)

-- Delete and capture deleted rows
DELETE FROM expired_tokens
WHERE expires_at < now()
RETURNING *;
-- Returns all deleted tokens for audit logging

-- Upsert with RETURNING
INSERT INTO configs (key, value)
VALUES ('timeout', '30')
ON CONFLICT (key) DO UPDATE SET value = EXCLUDED.value
RETURNING *, (xmax = 0) AS was_inserted;
-- xmax = 0 means it was inserted, otherwise updated

-- Chain with CTE
WITH deleted AS (
    DELETE FROM orders WHERE status = 'cancelled' RETURNING *
)
INSERT INTO order_archive SELECT * FROM deleted;
```

title: SET NOT NULL requires a full table scan and lock
deadlock detected — ERROR 40P01
could not serialize access due to concurrent update (serialization failure)
body:
With `REPEATABLE READ` or `SERIALIZABLE` isolation, concurrent modifications to the same row cause serialization failures that must be retried.

```
ERROR: could not serialize access due to concurrent update
SQLSTATE: 40001
```

```sql
-- This happens with:
BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;
UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = 1;
-- Another transaction updated id=1 and committed between our BEGIN and UPDATE
COMMIT;  -- ERROR!

-- Fix: application-level retry
-- The ENTIRE transaction must be retried, not just the failed statement
```

```python
import asyncpg

async def transfer(pool, from_id, to_id, amount):
    for attempt in range(5):
        try:
            async with pool.acquire() as conn:
                async with conn.transaction(
                    isolation='repeatable_read'
                ):
                    await conn.execute(
                        'UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - $1 WHERE id = $2',
                        amount, from_id
                    )
                    await conn.execute(
                        'UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + $1 WHERE id = $2',
                        amount, to_id
                    )
            return  # success
        except asyncpg.SerializationError:
            await asyncio.sleep(random.uniform(0.01, 0.1))
    raise RuntimeError("Transfer failed after 5 retries")
```

Serialization failures are expected behavior, not bugs. Any application using REPEATABLE READ or SERIALIZABLE must have retry logic.

title: too many connections for role — connection pool exhaustion
body:
PostgreSQL has hard limits on connections per user and total. Exceeding them gives `FATAL: too many connections`.

```
FATAL: too many connections for role "myapp"
FATAL: remaining connection slots are reserved for non-replication superuser connections
```

```sql
-- Check current connections
SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity;
SELECT count(*), usename, state
FROM pg_stat_activity
GROUP BY usename, state
ORDER BY count DESC;

-- Check limits
SHOW max_connections;  -- default: 100
SELECT rolconnlimit FROM pg_roles WHERE rolname = 'myapp';

-- Increase per-role limit
ALTER ROLE myapp CONNECTION LIMIT 50;

-- Kill idle connections
SELECT pg_terminate_backend(pid)
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE state = 'idle'
  AND state_change < now() - interval '10 minutes'
  AND pid != pg_backend_pid();
```

The real fix is connection pooling with PgBouncer:
```ini
# pgbouncer.ini
[databases]
mydb = host=localhost dbname=mydb

[pgbouncer]
max_client_conn = 1000  # app connections
default_pool_size = 25  # actual PG connections
pool_mode = transaction
```

Rule: each PostgreSQL connection uses ~10MB RAM. 100 connections = 1GB just for connections. Use PgBouncer to multiplex 1000s of app connections onto 25-50 actual connections.

title: value too long for type character varying(N) — column truncation
body:
PostgreSQL enforces `varchar(N)` strictly — it won't silently truncate like MySQL. You get an error if the value exceeds the length.

```
ERROR: value too long for type character varying(50)
```

```sql
-- This fails:
INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES (repeat('a', 51));  -- name is varchar(50)

-- Fixes:
-- 1. Increase column size (instant, no table rewrite)
ALTER TABLE users ALTER COLUMN name TYPE varchar(255);

-- 2. Use text instead (no length limit, same performance)
ALTER TABLE users ALTER COLUMN name TYPE text;
-- text and varchar have identical performance in PostgreSQL!
-- varchar(N) only adds a check constraint

-- 3. Add a check constraint for validation
ALTER TABLE users ADD CONSTRAINT name_length CHECK (length(name) <= 255);

-- 4. Truncate in application or query
INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES (left(very_long_name, 50));

-- Best practice: use text for all string columns
-- Add CHECK constraints for business logic validation
CREATE TABLE users (
    name text NOT NULL CHECK (length(name) BETWEEN 1 AND 255),
    email text NOT NULL CHECK (email ~* '^.+@.+\..+$'),
    bio text CHECK (length(bio) <= 5000)
);
```

In PostgreSQL, `text`, `varchar`, and `varchar(N)` have the same underlying storage. There's no performance reason to use `varchar(N)` over `text` with a CHECK constraint.

title: column reference is ambiguous in JOIN queries
body:
When two joined tables have columns with the same name, PostgreSQL requires you to qualify the column name.

```
ERROR: column reference "id" is ambiguous
LINE 1: SELECT id FROM orders JOIN users ON orders.user_id = users.id
```

```sql
-- WRONG: ambiguous column
SELECT id, name, created_at
FROM orders o
JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.id;
-- ERROR: both tables have 'id' and 'created_at'

-- FIX: qualify all ambiguous columns
SELECT o.id AS order_id, u.name, o.created_at
FROM orders o
JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.id;

-- Best practice: always qualify columns in JOINs
SELECT
    o.id AS order_id,
    o.total,
    o.created_at AS order_date,
    u.id AS user_id,
    u.name,
    u.email
FROM orders o
JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.id;

-- USING clause auto-deduplicates shared column names
SELECT * FROM orders JOIN users USING (id);  -- single 'id' column
-- But this only works if the join column has the same name in both tables
```

Always use table aliases and qualify column names in joins. This also makes queries more readable and maintainable.

title: current transaction is aborted — commands ignored until end of transaction
body:
After any error in a PostgreSQL transaction, ALL subsequent commands fail with this error until you `ROLLBACK`.

```
ERROR: current transaction is aborted, commands ignored until end of transaction block
```

```sql
BEGIN;
INSERT INTO users (email) VALUES ('bad');  -- fails: constraint violation
SELECT * FROM users;  -- ERROR: transaction is aborted!
COMMIT;  -- implicitly becomes ROLLBACK

-- Fix: use SAVEPOINT for error recovery
BEGIN;
SAVEPOINT sp1;
INSERT INTO users (email) VALUES ('bad');  -- fails
ROLLBACK TO sp1;  -- recover
INSERT INTO users (email) VALUES ('good@example.com');  -- works!
COMMIT;
```

```python
# Application fix: handle transaction state properly
try:
    await conn.execute('INSERT INTO users ...')
except asyncpg.UniqueViolationError:
    # Transaction is now aborted!
    # Must rollback before doing anything else
    await conn.execute('ROLLBACK')
    # Or better: use conn.transaction() context manager

# Best practice: use context manager
async with conn.transaction():
    await conn.execute('INSERT INTO users ...')
    # auto-rollback on exception, auto-commit on success
```

This behavior is by design — PostgreSQL guarantees transaction atomicity. Unlike MySQL, you can't continue a transaction after an error.

title: operator does not exist: jsonb = text — JSONB type mismatch
body:
JSONB values cannot be directly compared with text or integer values. You must extract as text or compare with JSONB literals.

```
ERROR: operator does not exist: jsonb = text
```

```sql
-- WRONG: comparing jsonb with text
SELECT * FROM events WHERE data->'type' = 'click';  -- ERROR!
-- data->'type' returns jsonb '"click"', not text 'click'

-- Fix 1: Extract as text with ->>
SELECT * FROM events WHERE data->>'type' = 'click';  -- works

-- Fix 2: Compare with jsonb literal
SELECT * FROM events WHERE data->'type' = '"click"'::jsonb;  -- works

-- WRONG: comparing jsonb number with integer
SELECT * FROM events WHERE data->'count' > 5;  -- ERROR!

-- Fix: cast properly
SELECT * FROM events WHERE (data->>'count')::int > 5;
-- Or use jsonb comparison
SELECT * FROM events WHERE data->'count' > '5'::jsonb;

-- WRONG: IN clause with jsonb
SELECT * FROM events WHERE data->'status' IN ('active', 'pending');  -- ERROR!

-- Fix:
SELECT * FROM events WHERE data->>'status' IN ('active', 'pending');
```

Remember: `->` returns jsonb, `->>` returns text. Use `->>` when comparing with text values, `->` when comparing with jsonb values or doing containment queries.

title: cannot drop table because other objects depend on it
body:
Foreign keys, views, and functions that reference a table prevent it from being dropped. Use `CASCADE` to drop everything, but be careful.

```
ERROR: cannot drop table users because other objects depend on it
DETAIL: constraint orders_user_id_fkey on table orders depends on table users
         view active_users depends on table users
```

```sql
-- See all dependencies
SELECT
    dependent_ns.nspname AS dependent_schema,
    dependent_view.relname AS dependent_object,
    source_ns.nspname AS source_schema,
    source_table.relname AS source_table
FROM pg_depend
JOIN pg_rewrite ON pg_depend.objid = pg_rewrite.oid
JOIN pg_class AS dependent_view ON pg_rewrite.ev_class = dependent_view.oid
JOIN pg_class AS source_table ON pg_depend.refobjid = source_table.oid
JOIN pg_namespace AS dependent_ns ON dependent_view.relnamespace = dependent_ns.oid
JOIN pg_namespace AS source_ns ON source_table.relnamespace = source_ns.oid
WHERE source_table.relname = 'users';

-- Drop with CASCADE (drops ALL dependent objects!)
DROP TABLE users CASCADE;
-- WARNING: this drops foreign keys, views, functions referencing users

-- Safer: drop dependents explicitly first
DROP VIEW IF EXISTS active_users;
ALTER TABLE orders DROP CONSTRAINT orders_user_id_fkey;
DROP TABLE users;
```

Never use `CASCADE` in production without checking dependencies first. It can silently drop views and constraints you didn't expect.

title: canceling statement due to statement timeout
body:
When `statement_timeout` is set, queries that exceed the limit are automatically killed. This is a safety net, not a bug.

```
ERROR: canceling statement due to statement timeout
```

```sql
-- Check current timeout
SHOW statement_timeout;  -- default: 0 (no limit)

-- Set per-session timeout
SET statement_timeout = '30s';

-- Set per-transaction timeout
SET LOCAL statement_timeout = '60s';  -- only for current transaction

-- Set for specific user/database
ALTER ROLE app_user SET statement_timeout = '30s';
ALTER DATABASE mydb SET statement_timeout = '60s';

-- Override for specific long-running queries
BEGIN;
SET LOCAL statement_timeout = '300s';  -- 5 min for this query
SELECT * FROM big_table WHERE complex_condition;
COMMIT;  -- timeout resets

-- For migrations that need more time
SET LOCAL statement_timeout = '0';  -- disable for this transaction
ALTER TABLE big_table ADD COLUMN ...;
COMMIT;

-- Check which queries are being killed
SELECT * FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE wait_event = 'Timeout'
  AND state = 'active';
```

Set `statement_timeout` to 30-60 seconds for web applications. Use explicit overrides for migrations and batch jobs.

title: no unique or exclusion constraint matching the ON CONFLICT specification
body:
The `ON CONFLICT` (upsert) clause requires an exact match with a unique or exclusion constraint. A missing or mismatched constraint causes this error.

```
ERROR: there is no unique or exclusion constraint matching the ON CONFLICT specification
```

```sql
-- Table with composite unique
CREATE TABLE user_settings (
    user_id int,
    key text,
    value text,
    UNIQUE (user_id, key)
);

-- WRONG: only specifying one column of composite unique
INSERT INTO user_settings (user_id, key, value) VALUES (1, 'theme', 'dark')
ON CONFLICT (user_id) DO UPDATE SET value = EXCLUDED.value;
-- ERROR: no matching constraint

-- CORRECT: specify all columns of the composite unique
INSERT INTO user_settings (user_id, key, value) VALUES (1, 'theme', 'dark')
ON CONFLICT (user_id, key) DO UPDATE SET value = EXCLUDED.value;

-- With partial unique index, use the constraint name
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_active_email ON users (email) WHERE deleted_at IS NULL;

-- Must reference by constraint name for partial indexes
INSERT INTO users (email, name) VALUES ('a@b.com', 'Alice')
ON CONFLICT (email) WHERE deleted_at IS NULL
DO UPDATE SET name = EXCLUDED.name;

-- Or use ON CONFLICT ON CONSTRAINT
INSERT INTO user_settings (user_id, key, value) VALUES (1, 'theme', 'dark')
ON CONFLICT ON CONSTRAINT user_settings_user_id_key_key
DO UPDATE SET value = EXCLUDED.value;
```

title: relation does not exist — schema search path issue
body:
When a table exists but queries fail with `relation does not exist`, the issue is usually the `search_path` not including the table's schema.

```
ERROR: relation "my_table" does not exist
```

```sql
-- Check which schema the table is in
SELECT schemaname, tablename
FROM pg_tables
WHERE tablename = 'my_table';

-- Check current search path
SHOW search_path;  -- default: "$user", public

-- Fix 1: use fully qualified name
SELECT * FROM myschema.my_table;

-- Fix 2: add schema to search path
SET search_path TO myschema, public;

-- Fix 3: set permanently for user
ALTER ROLE myapp SET search_path TO myschema, public;

-- Common causes:
-- 1. Table in non-public schema
-- 2. Case sensitivity: CREATE TABLE "MyTable" vs my_table
SELECT * FROM "MyTable";  -- quotes preserve case
SELECT * FROM MyTable;     -- converted to lowercase: mytable

-- 3. Table created in a transaction that was rolled back
-- 4. Wrong database (connected to wrong db)
SELECT current_database();
```

Always use lowercase, unquoted identifiers in PostgreSQL. If you must use mixed case, you'll need double quotes everywhere, which is painful.

title: permission denied for table — role privileges not granted
out of shared memory — too many locks from partitioned operations
body:
Operations on partitioned tables acquire locks on every partition. If you have hundreds of partitions, you can exhaust `max_locks_per_transaction`.

```
ERROR: out of shared memory
HINT: You might need to increase max_locks_per_transaction.
```

```sql
-- Check current setting
SHOW max_locks_per_transaction;  -- default: 64

-- Each partition needs its own lock
-- Table with 500 partitions + their indexes = 1000+ locks per query!

-- Fix: increase max_locks_per_transaction (requires restart)
-- postgresql.conf:
-- max_locks_per_transaction = 256

-- Or reduce partition count:
-- Instead of daily partitions (365/year), use monthly (12/year)
-- Instead of monthly, use quarterly (4/year)

-- Check lock usage
SELECT count(*) FROM pg_locks WHERE pid = pg_backend_pid();

-- Check which queries hold many locks
SELECT pid, count(*) AS lock_count
FROM pg_locks
GROUP BY pid
ORDER BY lock_count DESC;
```

The formula is: `max_locks_per_transaction * (max_connections + max_prepared_transactions)` = total lock table slots. With heavy partitioning, set `max_locks_per_transaction` to at least 2x your partition count.

title: duplicate key value violates unique constraint on concurrent INSERT
body:
Concurrent inserts can hit unique constraint violations even with `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT` if not handled properly.

```
ERROR: duplicate key value violates unique constraint "users_email_key"
DETAIL: Key (email)=(alice@example.com) already exists.
```

```sql
-- Upsert pattern that handles concurrent inserts
INSERT INTO users (email, name)
VALUES ('alice@example.com', 'Alice')
ON CONFLICT (email) DO UPDATE SET name = EXCLUDED.name
RETURNING *;

-- But ON CONFLICT can still race with DELETE!
-- Session A: INSERT ON CONFLICT → finds no conflict → inserts
-- Session B: INSERT ON CONFLICT → finds no conflict → inserts
-- One of them gets duplicate key error!

-- Robust solution: retry loop
WITH upsert AS (
    INSERT INTO users (email, name)
    VALUES ('alice@example.com', 'Alice')
    ON CONFLICT (email) DO UPDATE SET name = EXCLUDED.name
    RETURNING *
)
SELECT * FROM upsert
UNION ALL
SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = 'alice@example.com'
LIMIT 1;
```

```python
# Application-level retry for concurrent upserts
async def upsert_user(conn, email, name):
    for _ in range(3):
        try:
            return await conn.fetchrow(
                "INSERT INTO users (email, name) VALUES ($1, $2) "
                "ON CONFLICT (email) DO UPDATE SET name = $2 RETURNING *",
                email, name
            )
        except asyncpg.UniqueViolationError:
            continue  # retry
    raise RuntimeError("Upsert failed after retries")
```

title: SSL connection is required — SSL not configured on client
body:
Many managed PostgreSQL services require SSL connections. Without proper SSL config, connections are rejected.

```
FATAL: no pg_hba.conf entry for host "1.2.3.4", user "myapp", database "mydb", SSL off
-- or
FATAL: SSL connection is required
```

```python
# Python asyncpg with SSL
import asyncpg
import ssl

# Simple (verify-ca)
conn = await asyncpg.connect(
    'postgresql://user:pass@host/db',
    ssl='require'  # or 'verify-ca' or 'verify-full'
)

# With custom CA certificate
ssl_ctx = ssl.create_default_context(cafile='/path/to/ca.crt')
ssl_ctx.check_hostname = True
conn = await asyncpg.connect(
    'postgresql://user:pass@host/db',
    ssl=ssl_ctx
)

# SQLAlchemy with SSL
from sqlalchemy import create_engine
engine = create_engine(
    'postgresql://user:pass@host/db',
    connect_args={'sslmode': 'require'}
)

# Connection string with SSL
# postgresql://user:pass@host/db?sslmode=require
# sslmode options: disable, allow, prefer, require, verify-ca, verify-full
```

For production, use `sslmode=verify-full` which validates both the certificate and hostname. `sslmode=require` encrypts but doesn't verify the server identity.

title: WAL disk space exhaustion from replication slot lag
body:
Unused or lagging replication slots prevent PostgreSQL from recycling WAL (Write-Ahead Log) files, eventually filling the disk.

```
ERROR: could not extend file: No space left on device
PANIC: could not write to file: No space left on device
```

```sql
-- Check replication slot lag
SELECT
    slot_name,
    slot_type,
    active,
    pg_size_pretty(
        pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn)
    ) AS lag_size
FROM pg_replication_slots
ORDER BY pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn) DESC;

-- Drop inactive slots that are causing WAL accumulation
SELECT pg_drop_replication_slot('unused_slot_name');

-- Check WAL disk usage
SELECT pg_size_pretty(sum(size)) AS wal_size
FROM pg_ls_waldir();

-- Set max WAL size to prevent unbounded growth (PG 13+)
ALTER SYSTEM SET max_slot_wal_keep_size = '10GB';
SELECT pg_reload_conf();
-- Slots exceeding this limit are invalidated

-- Monitor WAL production rate
SELECT pg_current_wal_lsn();
-- Wait 1 minute, then:
SELECT pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), 'previous_lsn') / 1024 / 1024 AS mb_per_min;
```

Always set `max_slot_wal_keep_size` in production to prevent disk exhaustion from forgotten replication slots (common with logical replication and CDC tools like Debezium).

title: invalid input syntax for type integer — implicit cast failure
body:
PostgreSQL is strict about type casting. Unlike MySQL, it won't silently convert strings to numbers.

```
ERROR: invalid input syntax for type integer: "abc"
```

```sql
-- FAILS: string to int
SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 'abc';
-- ERROR: invalid input syntax for type integer

-- FAILS: casting non-numeric string
SELECT 'abc'::integer;

-- Safe casting with error handling
CREATE FUNCTION safe_cast_int(text) RETURNS integer AS $$
BEGIN
    RETURN $1::integer;
EXCEPTION WHEN invalid_text_representation THEN
    RETURN NULL;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql IMMUTABLE;

SELECT safe_cast_int('123');  -- 123
SELECT safe_cast_int('abc');  -- NULL

-- Common scenario: API sends string ID
-- Application should validate/cast before querying
SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1::integer;  -- explicit cast

-- JSONB number extraction
SELECT (data->>'count')::integer FROM events;  -- ->> returns text, must cast
SELECT (data->'count')::integer FROM events;  -- ERROR: can't cast jsonb to int
-- Fix: always use ->> to get text, then cast
```

title: lock timeout — waiting too long to acquire lock
body:
When `lock_timeout` is set, queries waiting longer than the limit for a lock are killed. This prevents cascading lock waits.

```
ERROR: canceling statement due to lock timeout
```

```sql
-- Set lock timeout (separate from statement_timeout)
SET lock_timeout = '5s';  -- fail fast instead of waiting

-- Common scenario: migration blocked by long query
SET LOCAL lock_timeout = '3s';
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN new_col text;  -- needs AccessExclusive lock
-- If any query is running on 'users', this waits (and times out)

-- Check what's blocking
SELECT
    blocked.pid AS blocked_pid,
    blocked.query AS blocked_query,
    blocking.pid AS blocking_pid,
    blocking.query AS blocking_query
FROM pg_stat_activity blocked
JOIN pg_locks bl ON bl.pid = blocked.pid
JOIN pg_locks kl ON bl.locktype = kl.locktype
    AND bl.database IS NOT DISTINCT FROM kl.database
    AND bl.relation IS NOT DISTINCT FROM kl.relation
    AND bl.page IS NOT DISTINCT FROM kl.page
    AND bl.tuple IS NOT DISTINCT FROM kl.tuple
    AND bl.pid != kl.pid
JOIN pg_stat_activity blocking ON kl.pid = blocking.pid
WHERE NOT bl.granted;

-- Safe migration pattern
SET LOCAL lock_timeout = '3s';
-- Retry migration in a loop until it acquires the lock
-- During brief gaps in traffic, the lock can be acquired
```

Set `lock_timeout = '3s'` for migrations. This prevents a long-running migration from blocking all other queries that pile up behind it.

title: Upsert pattern with ON CONFLICT and RETURNING
body:
The upsert pattern (INSERT or UPDATE if exists) is essential for idempotent operations and sync scenarios.

```sql
-- Basic upsert
INSERT INTO users (email, name, updated_at)
VALUES ('alice@example.com', 'Alice', now())
ON CONFLICT (email)
DO UPDATE SET
    name = EXCLUDED.name,
    updated_at = EXCLUDED.updated_at
RETURNING *, (xmax = 0) AS inserted;
-- xmax = 0 means INSERT, otherwise UPDATE

-- Upsert with conditional update (only if changed)
INSERT INTO configs (key, value, version)
VALUES ('timeout', '30', 1)
ON CONFLICT (key)
DO UPDATE SET
    value = EXCLUDED.value,
    version = configs.version + 1
WHERE configs.value IS DISTINCT FROM EXCLUDED.value
RETURNING *;
-- Only updates if value actually changed

-- Bulk upsert
INSERT INTO products (sku, name, price)
VALUES
    ('A001', 'Widget', 9.99),
    ('A002', 'Gadget', 19.99),
    ('A003', 'Doohickey', 4.99)
ON CONFLICT (sku)
DO UPDATE SET
    name = EXCLUDED.name,
    price = EXCLUDED.price;

-- DO NOTHING variant (insert if not exists)
INSERT INTO user_visits (user_id, page, visited_at)
VALUES (1, '/home', now())
ON CONFLICT (user_id, page) DO NOTHING;
```

title: Lateral join for top-N per group queries
body:
LATERAL joins are PostgreSQL's most powerful feature for correlated subqueries — especially for getting the top N rows per group.

```sql
-- Top 3 orders per user (using LATERAL)
SELECT u.id, u.name, o.id AS order_id, o.total, o.created_at
FROM users u
CROSS JOIN LATERAL (
    SELECT *
    FROM orders
    WHERE orders.user_id = u.id
    ORDER BY created_at DESC
    LIMIT 3
) o;

-- vs window function approach (less efficient for large datasets)
SELECT * FROM (
    SELECT u.name, o.*,
        row_number() OVER (PARTITION BY o.user_id ORDER BY o.created_at DESC) AS rn
    FROM orders o
    JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.id
) sub WHERE rn <= 3;

-- LATERAL with LEFT JOIN (include users with no orders)
SELECT u.id, u.name, o.total
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN LATERAL (
    SELECT total FROM orders
    WHERE user_id = u.id
    ORDER BY created_at DESC
    LIMIT 1
) o ON true;

-- LATERAL with generate_series for time bucketing
SELECT bucket, count(e.id)
FROM generate_series(
    '2024-01-01'::date,
    '2024-01-31'::date,
    '1 day'::interval
) bucket
LEFT JOIN LATERAL (
    SELECT id FROM events
    WHERE created_at >= bucket AND created_at < bucket + '1 day'::interval
) e ON true
GROUP BY bucket
ORDER BY bucket;
```

LATERAL joins are like for-each loops in SQL. The subquery can reference columns from preceding FROM items, which is not possible with regular joins.

title: Advisory locks for application-level distributed locking
body:
Advisory locks let you implement application-level locking without touching real tables. They're essential for job queues and singleton operations.

```sql
-- Session-level advisory lock (held until session ends or explicitly released)
SELECT pg_advisory_lock(12345);  -- blocks if another session holds it
-- ... do exclusive work ...
SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(12345);

-- Try lock (non-blocking)
SELECT pg_try_advisory_lock(12345);  -- returns true/false immediately

-- Transaction-level advisory lock (released at COMMIT/ROLLBACK)
BEGIN;
SELECT pg_advisory_xact_lock(12345);
-- ... do work ...
COMMIT;  -- lock automatically released

-- Two-key locks for resource-specific locking
-- Lock a specific user for processing
SELECT pg_advisory_xact_lock(
    hashtext('user_processing'),  -- namespace
    42                             -- user_id
);
```

```python
# Python pattern for singleton jobs
async def run_exclusive_job(conn, job_name: str):
    lock_id = hash(job_name) % (2**31)  # advisory locks are int32
    acquired = await conn.fetchval(
        'SELECT pg_try_advisory_lock($1)', lock_id
    )
    if not acquired:
        return  # another instance is running
    try:
        await do_job()
    finally:
        await conn.fetchval('SELECT pg_advisory_unlock($1)', lock_id)
```

Advisory locks are free-form — PostgreSQL doesn't enforce what they protect. They're purely cooperative between application instances.

title: Recursive CTE for tree and graph traversal
body:
Recursive CTEs handle hierarchical data like org charts, comment threads, and bill of materials.

```sql
-- Adjacency list table
CREATE TABLE categories (
    id int PRIMARY KEY,
    name text NOT NULL,
    parent_id int REFERENCES categories(id)
);

-- Get full path from leaf to root
WITH RECURSIVE category_tree AS (
    -- Base case: start from a specific category
    SELECT id, name, parent_id, ARRAY[name] AS path, 0 AS depth
    FROM categories
    WHERE id = 42

    UNION ALL

    -- Recursive case: walk up to parent
    SELECT c.id, c.name, c.parent_id,
        c.name || ct.path,  -- prepend parent name
        ct.depth + 1
    FROM categories c
    JOIN category_tree ct ON c.id = ct.parent_id
)
SELECT * FROM category_tree ORDER BY depth DESC;
-- Result: Electronics > Computers > Laptops > Gaming Laptops

-- Get all descendants of a category
WITH RECURSIVE descendants AS (
    SELECT id, name, parent_id, 0 AS depth
    FROM categories WHERE id = 1  -- root

    UNION ALL

    SELECT c.id, c.name, c.parent_id, d.depth + 1
    FROM categories c
    JOIN descendants d ON c.parent_id = d.id
    WHERE d.depth < 10  -- safety limit!
)
SELECT * FROM descendants;

-- Cycle detection
WITH RECURSIVE tree AS (
    SELECT id, parent_id, ARRAY[id] AS visited, false AS has_cycle
    FROM categories WHERE id = 1

    UNION ALL

    SELECT c.id, c.parent_id,
        t.visited || c.id,
        c.id = ANY(t.visited)
    FROM categories c
    JOIN tree t ON c.parent_id = t.id
    WHERE NOT t.has_cycle
)
SELECT * FROM tree;
```

Always include a depth limit in recursive CTEs to prevent infinite loops from circular references.

title: Window functions for running totals, rankings, and gaps
body:
Window functions perform calculations across related rows without collapsing them. They're essential for analytics queries.

```sql
-- Running total
SELECT
    date,
    revenue,
    SUM(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS running_total,
    SUM(revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY extract(month from date) ORDER BY date) AS monthly_running
FROM daily_revenue;

-- Ranking (row_number vs rank vs dense_rank)
SELECT
    name, score,
    row_number() OVER (ORDER BY score DESC) AS row_num,  -- 1,2,3,4
    rank() OVER (ORDER BY score DESC) AS rank,           -- 1,2,2,4
    dense_rank() OVER (ORDER BY score DESC) AS dense     -- 1,2,2,3
FROM players;

-- Lag/Lead for comparing with previous/next row
SELECT
    date,
    revenue,
    LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS prev_day,
    revenue - LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS daily_change,
    ROUND(100.0 * (revenue - LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY date))
        / LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY date), 2) AS pct_change
FROM daily_revenue;

-- Moving average
SELECT
    date,
    revenue,
    AVG(revenue) OVER (
        ORDER BY date
        ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    ) AS seven_day_avg
FROM daily_revenue;

-- Gap detection (find missing dates)
SELECT
    date,
    LEAD(date) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS next_date,
    LEAD(date) OVER (ORDER BY date) - date AS gap_days
FROM daily_revenue
HAVING LEAD(date) OVER (ORDER BY date) - date > 1;
```

title: JSONB document storage with type-safe querying
body:
Using JSONB as a flexible document store while maintaining type safety and query performance.

```sql
-- Table with structured + flexible columns
CREATE TABLE events (
    id bigint GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
    type text NOT NULL,
    data jsonb NOT NULL DEFAULT '{}',
    created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

-- Indexes for common access patterns
CREATE INDEX idx_events_type ON events (type);
CREATE INDEX idx_events_data ON events USING gin (data jsonb_path_ops);
CREATE INDEX idx_events_user ON events ((data->>'user_id'));

-- Type-safe JSONB queries
SELECT
    id,
    type,
    data->>'user_id' AS user_id,
    (data->>'amount')::numeric AS amount,
    (data->'items')::jsonb AS items,
    jsonb_array_length(data->'items') AS item_count
FROM events
WHERE type = 'purchase'
  AND data @> '{"status": "completed"}'
  AND (data->>'amount')::numeric > 100
  AND data->'items' @> '[{"sku": "WIDGET-1"}]'
ORDER BY created_at DESC;

-- Aggregate JSONB data
SELECT
    data->>'category' AS category,
    count(*) AS event_count,
    avg((data->>'amount')::numeric) AS avg_amount
FROM events
WHERE type = 'purchase'
GROUP BY data->>'category'
ORDER BY event_count DESC;

-- Update nested JSONB
UPDATE events SET data = jsonb_set(
    data,
    '{status}',  -- path
    '"processed"'::jsonb  -- new value
)
WHERE id = 1;

-- Deep merge
UPDATE events SET data = data || '{"processed": true, "version": 2}'::jsonb
WHERE id = 1;
```

title: Zero-downtime migration checklist for PostgreSQL
Batch processing with cursor-based pagination
Time-series partitioning with automatic partition management
Connection pool monitoring and tuning
body:
Monitor PostgreSQL connection health and tune pool settings based on actual usage patterns.

```sql
-- Current connection overview
SELECT
    state,
    count(*),
    round(100.0 * count(*) / sum(count(*)) over(), 2) as pct
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE backend_type = 'client backend'
GROUP BY state;

-- Idle connections wasting resources
SELECT
    pid,
    usename,
    application_name,
    state,
    now() - state_change AS idle_duration,
    query
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE state = 'idle'
ORDER BY state_change;

-- Connection limit vs usage
SELECT
    max_conn.setting::int AS max_connections,
    count(sa.pid) AS current_connections,
    max_conn.setting::int - count(sa.pid) AS available
FROM pg_stat_activity sa
CROSS JOIN pg_settings max_conn
WHERE max_conn.name = 'max_connections'
GROUP BY max_conn.setting;

-- Recommended pool sizing formula:
-- connections = ((core_count * 2) + effective_spindle_count)
-- For 4-core server with SSD: (4 * 2) + 1 = 9 connections
-- Each connection ≈ 10MB RAM

-- Auto-kill idle connections
ALTER SYSTEM SET idle_in_transaction_session_timeout = '300s';
ALTER SYSTEM SET idle_session_timeout = '3600s';  -- PG 14+
SELECT pg_reload_conf();
```

Formula: fewer connections = better performance in most cases. 25-50 actual database connections (via PgBouncer) typically outperform 200+ direct connections.

title: EXPLAIN ANALYZE reading guide — what to look for
body:
A systematic approach to reading EXPLAIN ANALYZE output to identify query performance bottlenecks.

```sql
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT)
SELECT u.name, count(o.id)
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
WHERE o.created_at > '2024-01-01'
GROUP BY u.name
ORDER BY count DESC
LIMIT 10;
```

What to check:
```
-- 1. Scan type
Seq Scan          → No index used (bad for large tables)
Index Scan        → Index used, fetches rows from heap
Index Only Scan   → Best! Data from index only (run VACUUM for visibility map)
Bitmap Index Scan → Multiple index conditions combined

-- 2. Row estimates vs actual
Rows Removed by Filter: 999000  → Index missing for this filter
(actual rows=10 estimated=10000) → Bad statistics, run ANALYZE

-- 3. Buffers (I/O)
Buffers: shared hit=100 read=5000 → 98% cache miss, need more RAM
Buffers: shared hit=5000 read=0   → All from cache, good

-- 4. Sort operations
Sort Method: external merge Disk: 50MB → Not enough work_mem
Sort Method: quicksort Memory: 1MB    → Fine
-- Fix: SET work_mem = '256MB';  (per-query)

-- 5. Join type
Nested Loop  → Good for small result sets, bad for large
Hash Join    → Good for large datasets, needs memory
Merge Join   → Good when both sides are sorted

-- 6. Total timing
Planning Time: 5.0ms   → Consider prepared statements if high
Execution Time: 100ms  → Acceptable for most queries
```

title: Row-level security for multi-tenant SaaS applications
body:
Implement tenant isolation using RLS policies so every query is automatically scoped to the current tenant.

```sql
-- Setup tenant column on all tables
CREATE TABLE orders (
    id bigint GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
    tenant_id int NOT NULL REFERENCES tenants(id),
    amount numeric NOT NULL,
    created_at timestamptz DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE INDEX idx_orders_tenant ON orders (tenant_id);

-- Enable RLS
ALTER TABLE orders ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
ALTER TABLE orders FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

-- Create policy
CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation ON orders
    USING (tenant_id = current_setting('app.tenant_id')::int)
    WITH CHECK (tenant_id = current_setting('app.tenant_id')::int);

-- Application middleware sets tenant context
-- In every request handler:
SET LOCAL app.tenant_id = '42';  -- SET LOCAL = transaction-scoped

-- All queries are automatically filtered!
SELECT * FROM orders;  -- only returns tenant 42's orders
INSERT INTO orders (tenant_id, amount) VALUES (42, 100);  -- allowed
INSERT INTO orders (tenant_id, amount) VALUES (99, 100);  -- blocked by WITH CHECK!
```

```python
# FastAPI middleware for tenant context
@app.middleware("http")
async def set_tenant(request: Request, call_next):
    tenant_id = get_tenant_from_token(request)
    async with db.acquire() as conn:
        await conn.execute(f"SET LOCAL app.tenant_id = '{tenant_id}'")
        request.state.db = conn
        return await call_next(request)
```

RLS provides defense-in-depth — even if application code has a bug, the database prevents cross-tenant data access.

title: Outbox pattern for reliable event publishing with PostgreSQL
body:
Store events in an outbox table within the same transaction as business logic, then publish them asynchronously for guaranteed delivery.

```sql
-- Outbox table
CREATE TABLE outbox (
    id bigint GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
    aggregate_type text NOT NULL,
    aggregate_id text NOT NULL,
    event_type text NOT NULL,
    payload jsonb NOT NULL,
    created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
    published_at timestamptz
);

CREATE INDEX idx_outbox_unpublished ON outbox (created_at)
    WHERE published_at IS NULL;

-- Business operation + event in same transaction
BEGIN;
UPDATE orders SET status = 'shipped' WHERE id = 123;

INSERT INTO outbox (aggregate_type, aggregate_id, event_type, payload)
VALUES (
    'order', '123', 'order.shipped',
    '{"order_id": 123, "shipped_at": "2024-01-15T10:00:00Z"}'
);
COMMIT;  -- both succeed or both fail

-- Publisher worker (polls and publishes)
WITH batch AS (
    SELECT id, event_type, payload
    FROM outbox
    WHERE published_at IS NULL
    ORDER BY created_at
    LIMIT 100
    FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED
)
UPDATE outbox SET published_at = now()
FROM batch WHERE outbox.id = batch.id
RETURNING batch.*;

-- Use LISTEN/NOTIFY for instant notification
CREATE FUNCTION notify_outbox() RETURNS trigger AS $$
BEGIN
    PERFORM pg_notify('outbox', NEW.event_type);
    RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE TRIGGER trg_outbox_notify
    AFTER INSERT ON outbox
    FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION notify_outbox();
```

The outbox pattern guarantees at-least-once delivery. The consumer must be idempotent (use the event ID for deduplication).

title: Full-text search with highlights and autocomplete
body:
Build a complete search experience with result highlighting, phrase matching, and prefix-based autocomplete.

```sql
-- Search with highlighted results
SELECT
    id,
    title,
    ts_headline('english', body, query,
        'StartSel=<mark>, StopSel=</mark>, MaxFragments=3, MaxWords=30'
    ) AS snippet,
    ts_rank_cd(search_vector, query) AS rank
FROM articles,
    websearch_to_tsquery('english', 'machine learning python') AS query
WHERE search_vector @@ query
ORDER BY rank DESC
LIMIT 20;

-- Autocomplete with prefix matching
SELECT DISTINCT word
FROM ts_stat('SELECT search_vector FROM articles')
WHERE word LIKE 'mach%'
ORDER BY nentry DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- Better autocomplete: trigram similarity
SELECT title,
    similarity(title, 'machin learnin') AS sim
FROM articles
WHERE title % 'machin learnin'  -- trigram similarity threshold
ORDER BY sim DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- Combined: fuzzy match + full-text search
SELECT
    title,
    ts_rank(search_vector, query) AS relevance,
    similarity(title, 'machine learning') AS title_sim
FROM articles,
    to_tsquery('english', 'machine & learning') AS query
WHERE search_vector @@ query
   OR title % 'machine learning'
ORDER BY relevance * 0.7 + title_sim * 0.3 DESC
LIMIT 20;
```

For production search, combine full-text search (word matching) with trigram similarity (typo tolerance). `websearch_to_tsquery` handles user input safely.

title: PostgreSQL job queue with SKIP LOCKED
body:
Build a reliable, concurrent job queue using `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` — no external dependencies needed.

```sql
-- Queue table
CREATE TABLE jobs (
    id bigint GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
    queue text NOT NULL DEFAULT 'default',
    payload jsonb NOT NULL,
    status text NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending',
    attempts int NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    max_attempts int NOT NULL DEFAULT 3,
    scheduled_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
    started_at timestamptz,
    completed_at timestamptz,
    error text
);

CREATE INDEX idx_jobs_pending ON jobs (scheduled_at)
    WHERE status = 'pending';

-- Claim a job (concurrent-safe)
WITH claimed AS (
    SELECT id FROM jobs
    WHERE status = 'pending'
      AND scheduled_at <= now()
      AND queue = 'default'
    ORDER BY scheduled_at
    LIMIT 1
    FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED  -- skip jobs claimed by other workers
)
UPDATE jobs SET
    status = 'running',
    started_at = now(),
    attempts = attempts + 1
FROM claimed
WHERE jobs.id = claimed.id
RETURNING jobs.*;

-- Complete a job
UPDATE jobs SET
    status = 'completed',
    completed_at = now()
WHERE id = $1;

-- Fail a job (with retry)
UPDATE jobs SET
    status = CASE
        WHEN attempts >= max_attempts THEN 'failed'
        ELSE 'pending'
    END,
    error = $2,
    scheduled_at = now() + (interval '1 second' * power(2, attempts))  -- exponential backoff
WHERE id = $1;

-- Clean old completed jobs
DELETE FROM jobs WHERE status = 'completed' AND completed_at < now() - interval '7 days';
```

`SKIP LOCKED` ensures multiple workers never process the same job. This pattern handles thousands of jobs per second.

title: JSONB path queries with jsonpath (PostgreSQL 12+)
body:
jsonpath provides SQL/JSON path expressions for querying JSONB documents — more powerful and readable than chained `->` operators.

```sql
-- Basic jsonpath queries
SELECT jsonb_path_query_first(data, '$.user.name') AS name
FROM events;

-- Filter within jsonpath
SELECT jsonb_path_query_array(
    data,
    '$.items[*] ? (@.price > 100)'
) AS expensive_items
FROM orders;

-- Existence check
SELECT * FROM events
WHERE data @? '$.tags[*] ? (@ == "urgent")';
-- @? returns boolean: does the path exist?

-- Comparison
SELECT * FROM events
WHERE data @@ '$.amount > 1000';
-- @@ returns boolean: does the predicate hold?

-- Extract all values matching a pattern
SELECT jsonb_path_query_array(
    '{"a": {"b": 1}, "c": {"b": 2}, "d": {"b": 3}}'::jsonb,
    '$.*.b'
);  -- [1, 2, 3]

-- With variables
SELECT * FROM events
WHERE data @? '$.items[*] ? (@.sku == $sku)'
    -- Pass variables:
    -- jsonb_path_exists(data, '$.items[*] ? (@.sku == $sku)', '{"sku": "WIDGET-1"}')
;

SELECT jsonb_path_query_array(
    data,
    '$.items[*] ? (@.price > $min_price)',
    '{"min_price": 50}'
)
FROM orders;
```

jsonpath is GIN-index compatible with `@?` and `@@` operators. It's the preferred way to query complex JSONB structures in PostgreSQL 12+.

title: Audit trail with temporal tables pattern
PostgreSQL connection configuration for production applications
body:
A complete PostgreSQL configuration template for production use with connection pooling, timeouts, and monitoring.

```sql
-- postgresql.conf essential settings

-- Memory
shared_buffers = '4GB'              -- 25% of RAM
effective_cache_size = '12GB'       -- 75% of RAM
work_mem = '64MB'                   -- per-sort/hash operation
maintenance_work_mem = '512MB'      -- for VACUUM, CREATE INDEX

-- WAL
wal_buffers = '64MB'
max_wal_size = '4GB'
min_wal_size = '1GB'
checkpoint_completion_target = 0.9

-- Query planner
random_page_cost = 1.1              -- SSD (default 4.0 for HDD)
effective_io_concurrency = 200      -- SSD (default 1 for HDD)

-- Connections
max_connections = 200               -- Use pgbouncer for more
idle_in_transaction_session_timeout = '300s'
statement_timeout = '60s'
lock_timeout = '10s'

-- Logging
log_min_duration_statement = 1000   -- log queries > 1s
log_checkpoints = on
log_lock_waits = on
log_temp_files = 0                  -- log all temp file usage

-- Autovacuum
autovacuum_max_workers = 4
autovacuum_vacuum_cost_limit = 800  -- more aggressive than default 200

-- Extensions
shared_preload_libraries = 'pg_stat_statements'
```

```ini
# pgbouncer.ini
[databases]
mydb = host=localhost dbname=mydb

[pgbouncer]
listen_port = 6432
pool_mode = transaction
max_client_conn = 2000
default_pool_size = 25
reserve_pool_size = 5

server_reset_query = DISCARD ALL
log_connections = 0
log_disconnections = 0
```
