title: PostgreSQL monitoring queries for daily health checks
body:
Essential monitoring queries to run daily on production PostgreSQL databases.

```sql
-- 1. Database size and growth
SELECT
    datname,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_database_size(datname)) AS size
FROM pg_database
ORDER BY pg_database_size(datname) DESC;

-- 2. Table sizes with bloat estimate
SELECT
    schemaname || '.' || tablename AS table,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(schemaname || '.' || tablename)) AS total,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_table_size(schemaname || '.' || tablename)) AS data,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_indexes_size(schemaname || '.' || tablename)) AS indexes,
    n_dead_tup AS dead_rows,
    last_autovacuum
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
ORDER BY pg_total_relation_size(schemaname || '.' || tablename) DESC
LIMIT 20;

-- 3. Unused indexes (candidates for removal)
SELECT
    schemaname || '.' || indexrelname AS index,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS size,
    idx_scan AS scans
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE idx_scan = 0
  AND indexrelname NOT LIKE '%pkey%'
  AND indexrelname NOT LIKE '%unique%'
ORDER BY pg_relation_size(indexrelid) DESC;

-- 4. Cache hit ratio (should be >99%)
SELECT
    sum(heap_blks_hit) / NULLIF(sum(heap_blks_hit) + sum(heap_blks_read), 0) AS ratio
FROM pg_statio_user_tables;

-- 5. Slow queries (requires pg_stat_statements)
SELECT
    query,
    calls,
    round(mean_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS avg_ms,
    round(total_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS total_ms
FROM pg_stat_statements
ORDER BY mean_exec_time DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- 6. Replication lag
SELECT
    client_addr,
    state,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), replay_lsn)) AS lag
FROM pg_stat_replication;
```

title: PostgreSQL backup and point-in-time recovery with pg_basebackup
body:
Set up continuous archiving and point-in-time recovery (PITR) for PostgreSQL.

```bash
# Enable WAL archiving (postgresql.conf)
# wal_level = replica
# archive_mode = on
# archive_command = 'cp %p /backup/wal/%f'

# Take base backup
pg_basebackup -h localhost -U replicator -D /backup/base \
  --checkpoint=fast --wal-method=stream --progress --verbose

# Restore to a specific point in time
# 1. Stop PostgreSQL
sudo systemctl stop postgresql

# 2. Move current data directory
mv /var/lib/postgresql/16/main /var/lib/postgresql/16/main.old

# 3. Restore base backup
cp -r /backup/base /var/lib/postgresql/16/main

# 4. Create recovery signal file
touch /var/lib/postgresql/16/main/recovery.signal

# 5. Configure recovery (postgresql.conf)
# restore_command = 'cp /backup/wal/%f %p'
# recovery_target_time = '2024-01-15 14:30:00'
# recovery_target_action = 'promote'

# 6. Start PostgreSQL
sudo systemctl start postgresql
# PostgreSQL replays WAL up to target time
```

```sql
-- Verify backup recoverability
SELECT pg_is_in_recovery();  -- true during recovery

-- After recovery completes
SELECT pg_is_in_recovery();  -- false

-- Check last recovered WAL position
SELECT pg_last_wal_replay_lsn();
SELECT pg_last_xact_replay_timestamp();
```

Test your backup/restore procedure regularly. An untested backup is not a backup.

title: PostgreSQL migration script template with safety guards
PostgreSQL JSONB manipulation functions cheat sheet
body:
Quick reference for all common JSONB operations — creation, querying, modification, and aggregation.

```sql
-- Creation
SELECT jsonb_build_object('name', 'Alice', 'age', 30);
-- {"age": 30, "name": "Alice"}

SELECT jsonb_build_array(1, 'two', true, null);
-- [1, "two", true, null]

SELECT to_jsonb(row(1, 'Alice'));
-- {"f1": 1, "f2": "Alice"}

-- Extraction
SELECT data->'key'        -- jsonb (preserves type)
SELECT data->>'key'       -- text
SELECT data#>'{a,b,c}'   -- nested path as jsonb
SELECT data#>>'{a,b,c}'  -- nested path as text
SELECT data->'arr'->0    -- array element (0-indexed)

-- Modification
SELECT data || '{"new_key": "value"}'::jsonb;  -- merge
SELECT data - 'key';                            -- remove key
SELECT data - '{key1,key2}'::text[];            -- remove multiple keys
SELECT data #- '{nested,key}';                  -- remove nested key
SELECT jsonb_set(data, '{key}', '"new_val"');   -- set value
SELECT jsonb_set(data, '{new}', '"val"', true); -- create if missing
SELECT jsonb_insert(data, '{arr,0}', '"first"');-- insert into array

-- Iteration
SELECT key, value FROM jsonb_each(data);         -- key-value pairs
SELECT key, value FROM jsonb_each_text(data);    -- text values
SELECT value FROM jsonb_array_elements(data);     -- array elements
SELECT value FROM jsonb_array_elements_text(data); -- text array elements

-- Aggregation
SELECT jsonb_agg(name) FROM users;               -- ["Alice", "Bob"]
SELECT jsonb_object_agg(key, value) FROM pairs;  -- {"k1": "v1", ...}

-- Comparison
SELECT data @> '{"key": "val"}'   -- contains
SELECT data <@ '{"key": "val"}'   -- contained by
SELECT data ? 'key'               -- key exists
SELECT data ?| array['a','b']     -- any key exists
SELECT data ?& array['a','b']     -- all keys exist
```

title: PostgreSQL window functions cheat sheet
body:
All window function patterns in one reference — ranking, aggregation, navigation, and framing.

```sql
-- Ranking functions
SELECT
    name, score,
    row_number() OVER w AS row_num,     -- 1,2,3,4,5
    rank() OVER w AS rank,               -- 1,2,2,4,5 (gaps)
    dense_rank() OVER w AS dense,        -- 1,2,2,3,4 (no gaps)
    ntile(4) OVER w AS quartile,         -- split into 4 groups
    percent_rank() OVER w AS pct_rank,   -- 0.0 to 1.0
    cume_dist() OVER w AS cume           -- cumulative distribution
FROM players
WINDOW w AS (ORDER BY score DESC);      -- named window

-- Navigation functions
SELECT
    date, value,
    LAG(value, 1) OVER w AS prev,        -- previous row
    LEAD(value, 1) OVER w AS next,       -- next row
    FIRST_VALUE(value) OVER w AS first,  -- first in window
    LAST_VALUE(value) OVER (
        ORDER BY date
        ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
    ) AS last,                            -- last (needs full frame!)
    NTH_VALUE(value, 3) OVER w AS third  -- 3rd value in window
FROM metrics
WINDOW w AS (ORDER BY date);

-- Aggregate window functions with framing
SELECT
    date, revenue,
    -- Running total
    SUM(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS running_sum,
    -- 7-day moving average
    AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY date ROWS 6 PRECEDING) AS avg_7d,
    -- Expanding average (all rows up to current)
    AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY date ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) AS expanding_avg,
    -- Centered window
    AVG(revenue) OVER (
        ORDER BY date ROWS BETWEEN 3 PRECEDING AND 3 FOLLOWING
    ) AS centered_avg
FROM daily_revenue;

-- Partition + order
SELECT
    department, name, salary,
    SUM(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY department) AS dept_total,
    salary::numeric / SUM(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY department) AS pct_of_dept,
    rank() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) AS dept_rank
FROM employees;
```

title: PostgreSQL index selection decision tree
body:
Choose the right index type based on your query pattern.

```sql
-- DECISION TREE:
-- Equality (=) or range (<, >, BETWEEN)? → B-tree (default)
CREATE INDEX idx ON t (col);
CREATE INDEX idx ON t (col1, col2);  -- composite for multi-column

-- Array containment (@>, &&)? → GIN
CREATE INDEX idx ON t USING gin (tags);

-- JSONB containment (@>)? → GIN
CREATE INDEX idx ON t USING gin (data);
CREATE INDEX idx ON t USING gin (data jsonb_path_ops);  -- smaller, @> only

-- Full-text search (@@)? → GIN
CREATE INDEX idx ON t USING gin (search_vector);

-- LIKE/ILIKE with leading wildcard? → GIN with trigram
CREATE EXTENSION pg_trgm;
CREATE INDEX idx ON t USING gin (col gin_trgm_ops);

-- Range types (&&, @>, <@)? → GiST
CREATE INDEX idx ON t USING gist (during);  -- tstzrange

-- Spatial/geometric? → GiST
CREATE INDEX idx ON t USING gist (location);  -- PostGIS

-- Time-series on append-only table? → BRIN
CREATE INDEX idx ON t USING brin (created_at);  -- 1000x smaller

-- COMPOUND STRATEGIES:
-- Frequently filtered subset? → Partial index
CREATE INDEX idx ON t (col) WHERE status = 'active';

-- Query only needs index columns? → Covering index
CREATE INDEX idx ON t (user_id) INCLUDE (name, email);
-- Enables index-only scans without heap access

-- Multiple columns with different operators? → Composite
CREATE INDEX idx ON t (status, created_at DESC);  -- for ORDER BY

-- Expression index for computed values
CREATE INDEX idx ON t (lower(email));  -- for case-insensitive search
CREATE INDEX idx ON t ((data->>'type'));  -- for JSONB field access
```

title: PostgreSQL common table expressions (CTE) patterns
body:
A collection of practical CTE patterns for complex queries — data transformation, deduplication, and reporting.

```sql
-- 1. Data pipeline with multiple steps
WITH
raw_events AS (
    SELECT * FROM events
    WHERE created_at >= current_date - 30
),
aggregated AS (
    SELECT
        user_id,
        count(*) AS event_count,
        count(DISTINCT date_trunc('day', created_at)) AS active_days
    FROM raw_events
    GROUP BY user_id
),
scored AS (
    SELECT *,
        CASE
            WHEN active_days >= 20 THEN 'power'
            WHEN active_days >= 10 THEN 'regular'
            ELSE 'casual'
        END AS user_tier
    FROM aggregated
)
SELECT user_tier, count(*), avg(event_count)::int AS avg_events
FROM scored GROUP BY user_tier;

-- 2. Deduplication (keep latest)
WITH ranked AS (
    SELECT *,
        row_number() OVER (PARTITION BY email ORDER BY created_at DESC) AS rn
    FROM users
)
DELETE FROM users WHERE id IN (
    SELECT id FROM ranked WHERE rn > 1
);

-- 3. INSERT with data from multiple sources
WITH new_users AS (
    INSERT INTO users (email, name)
    VALUES ('a@b.com', 'Alice')
    RETURNING id, email
),
default_settings AS (
    INSERT INTO user_settings (user_id, theme)
    SELECT id, 'dark' FROM new_users
    RETURNING user_id
)
SELECT * FROM new_users;

-- 4. Pivot / cross-tab
WITH monthly AS (
    SELECT
        product_id,
        extract(month from sold_at) AS month,
        sum(quantity) AS qty
    FROM sales
    WHERE sold_at >= '2024-01-01'
    GROUP BY 1, 2
)
SELECT
    product_id,
    sum(qty) FILTER (WHERE month = 1) AS jan,
    sum(qty) FILTER (WHERE month = 2) AS feb,
    sum(qty) FILTER (WHERE month = 3) AS mar
FROM monthly
GROUP BY product_id;
```

title: PostgreSQL FILTER clause for conditional aggregation
body:
The FILTER clause is a clean PostgreSQL-specific way to apply conditions to aggregate functions without CASE expressions.

```sql
-- Without FILTER (ugly CASE)
SELECT
    count(CASE WHEN status = 'active' THEN 1 END) AS active_count,
    count(CASE WHEN status = 'inactive' THEN 1 END) AS inactive_count,
    avg(CASE WHEN status = 'active' THEN score END) AS active_avg_score
FROM users;

-- With FILTER (clean)
SELECT
    count(*) FILTER (WHERE status = 'active') AS active_count,
    count(*) FILTER (WHERE status = 'inactive') AS inactive_count,
    avg(score) FILTER (WHERE status = 'active') AS active_avg_score,
    sum(revenue) FILTER (WHERE created_at >= current_date - 7) AS weekly_revenue,
    sum(revenue) FILTER (WHERE created_at >= current_date - 30) AS monthly_revenue
FROM users;

-- FILTER with GROUP BY for reports
SELECT
    date_trunc('month', created_at) AS month,
    count(*) AS total_orders,
    count(*) FILTER (WHERE status = 'completed') AS completed,
    count(*) FILTER (WHERE status = 'refunded') AS refunded,
    sum(amount) FILTER (WHERE status = 'completed') AS revenue,
    round(
        100.0 * count(*) FILTER (WHERE status = 'completed') / count(*),
        2
    ) AS completion_rate
FROM orders
GROUP BY month
ORDER BY month;

-- FILTER with array_agg
SELECT
    department,
    array_agg(name) FILTER (WHERE salary > 100000) AS high_earners,
    array_agg(name) FILTER (WHERE hire_date > current_date - 365) AS recent_hires
FROM employees
GROUP BY department;
```

The FILTER clause is SQL:2003 standard but many databases don't support it. It's more readable and often slightly faster than equivalent CASE expressions.

title: PostgreSQL array operations and functions reference
PostgreSQL table and index size analysis queries
body:
Queries to understand storage usage, identify bloated tables, and find optimization opportunities.

```sql
-- Complete table size breakdown
SELECT
    schemaname || '.' || tablename AS table_name,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(schemaname || '.' || tablename)) AS total,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_table_size(schemaname || '.' || tablename)) AS table_data,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_indexes_size(schemaname || '.' || tablename)) AS all_indexes,
    pg_size_pretty(
        pg_total_relation_size(schemaname || '.' || tablename) -
        pg_table_size(schemaname || '.' || tablename) -
        pg_indexes_size(schemaname || '.' || tablename)
    ) AS toast,
    n_live_tup AS live_rows,
    n_dead_tup AS dead_rows,
    CASE WHEN n_live_tup > 0
        THEN round(100.0 * n_dead_tup / n_live_tup, 2)
        ELSE 0
    END AS dead_pct
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
ORDER BY pg_total_relation_size(schemaname || '.' || tablename) DESC
LIMIT 20;

-- Individual index sizes
SELECT
    schemaname || '.' || indexrelname AS index_name,
    schemaname || '.' || tablename AS table_name,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS index_size,
    idx_scan AS scans_count,
    idx_tup_read AS rows_read,
    idx_tup_fetch AS rows_fetched
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
ORDER BY pg_relation_size(indexrelid) DESC
LIMIT 20;

-- Duplicate indexes (same columns)
SELECT
    a.indexrelid::regclass AS index_a,
    b.indexrelid::regclass AS index_b,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(a.indexrelid)) AS size_a,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(b.indexrelid)) AS size_b
FROM pg_index a
JOIN pg_index b ON a.indrelid = b.indrelid
    AND a.indexrelid < b.indexrelid
    AND a.indkey = b.indkey
WHERE a.indrelid::regclass::text NOT LIKE 'pg_%';

-- Total database size by schema
SELECT
    schemaname,
    pg_size_pretty(sum(pg_total_relation_size(schemaname || '.' || tablename))) AS total
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
GROUP BY schemaname
ORDER BY sum(pg_total_relation_size(schemaname || '.' || tablename)) DESC;
```
